description: Uterine lining sheds. Estrogen and progesterone are at their lowest. Prostaglandins trigger contractions causing cramps.

food.science: During menstruation, the body loses iron through blood. Anti-inflammatory foods reduce prostaglandin-driven cramping (University of Queensland, 2024).
versions: Focus on iron-rich foods like spinach, lentils, and lean red meat to replace what you lose during your period. Pair with vitamin C (orange juice, bell peppers) to boost absorption by up to 3x.
Your iron levels drop during your period. Lentil soup, tofu stir-fry, or a spinach salad with lemon dressing are simple ways to replenish — and adding vitamin C doubles absorption.
Iron loss is real this week. Dark leafy greens, legumes, and fortified cereals help. Always combine them with something citrusy — vitamin C is the key that unlocks iron absorption.

exercise.science: Light movement increases endorphins and reduces prostaglandin sensitivity. Intense exercise during low-hormone phase increases muscle soreness (systematic review, 78 studies, 2020).
versions: Gentle movement is your friend today. A 20-minute walk or restorative yoga can release endorphins that naturally reduce pain — without taxing your body further.
Your body is working hard. Skip the high-intensity workouts and opt for yin yoga, stretching, or a slow walk. Movement boosts endorphins and eases cramps without exhausting you.
Low-impact is the goal. Swimming, walking, or gentle yoga support circulation and mood without the soreness risk that comes with intense training when estrogen is at its lowest.

selfcare.science: Heat therapy increases blood flow and reduces uterine muscle spasms. Studies show 38-40°C heat patches are as effective as ibuprofen for mild cramps (BMJ, 2001).
versions: A heating pad on your lower abdomen is clinically backed — heat at 38-40°C relaxes uterine muscles as effectively as pain medication for mild cramps.
Warm baths and heat patches aren't just comfort — they're medicine. Heat dilates blood vessels, reduces spasms, and can work as well as ibuprofen for mild-to-moderate cramps.
Heat therapy works. Apply a warm pack to your lower belly for 20 minutes. Studies show it rivals ibuprofen for cramp relief by relaxing the uterine muscle directly.

food.science: Prostaglandins cause uterine contractions and cramps. Anti-inflammatory foods (omega-3s, magnesium, ginger) reduce prostaglandin production. Trans fats and refined sugar increase prostaglandins (Medical News Today, 2024).
versions: Your cramps are driven by prostaglandins — hormone-like compounds that contract the uterus. Fight back with omega-3s (salmon, walnuts, flaxseeds), magnesium-rich dark chocolate, and ginger tea. Avoid processed foods and sugar — they actively raise prostaglandin levels.

Severe cramps need an anti-inflammatory plate. Fatty fish, chia seeds, turmeric milk, and leafy greens block the prostaglandins causing your pain. Cut out caffeine, trans fats, and alcohol — they tighten blood vessels and make cramps worse.

Science is clear: what you eat affects cramp intensity. Omega-3 rich foods (walnuts, sardines, hemp seeds) directly compete with inflammatory prostaglandins. Add ginger — it inhibits prostaglandin synthesis similarly to NSAIDs. Ditch processed meat and sugar today.

exercise.science: Gentle movement is still recommended during severe cramps — complete rest can increase stiffness. Child's pose and legs-up-wall reduce pelvic tension (Yoga Journal, evidence-based review).
versions: Don't push through severe cramps with intense workouts — it can backfire. Instead, try child's pose, supine twists, or legs-up-the-wall for 10 minutes. These postures decompress the pelvis and ease cramping without strain.

Rest, but don't stay completely still. Gentle yoga specifically designed for cramps — child's pose, cat-cow, reclining bound angle — increases pelvic blood flow and reduces muscle spasm. Even 10 minutes makes a difference.

Your body needs gentleness today. Skip the gym. A slow 10-minute walk or yoga nidra (lying-down relaxation) activates the parasympathetic nervous system, reducing pain signals and calming the cramping.

selfcare.science: Heat at 38-40°C rivals ibuprofen for cramp relief. Magnesium supplements (200-400mg) reduce menstrual pain by inhibiting muscle contractions (Cochrane Review, 2023).
versions: Heat pad directly on your lower abdomen — keep it there for 20 minutes. This rivals ibuprofen for mild-to-moderate cramps. If you have magnesium glycinate, take 200-400mg tonight — it relaxes smooth muscle and is backed by a 2023 Cochrane review.

The most evidence-backed cramp combo: heat + magnesium. A heating pad relaxes uterine muscle spasms. Magnesium glycinate (200-400mg) reduces the intensity of contractions. Together they can significantly cut down on the need for pain meds.

For severe cramps, stack your relief: heat therapy + hydration + position. Lie on your side with knees pulled to chest (fetal position reduces abdominal tension), apply heat, and sip ginger or chamomile tea. Chamomile's glycine compound has muscle-relaxing properties.

food.science: Iron loss during menstruation directly causes fatigue. Non-anemic iron deficiency still reduces work capacity. Vitamin C triples non-heme iron absorption. B12 and folate support red blood cell production (Nova Southeastern University study).
versions: Fatigue during your period is often iron-driven — your body is losing iron with every drop of blood. Eat iron-rich foods: spinach, lentils, red meat, tofu. Critical: always pair them with vitamin C (squeeze lemon, eat a kiwi) — it triples your iron absorption.

Your exhaustion is real and physiological — iron drops as you bleed. Lentil dal, beef stir-fry, or a spinach-chickpea salad are your best friends right now. Add orange slices on the side — the vitamin C is what unlocks the iron into your bloodstream.

Low energy this week is your body talking. Iron is leaving with your period blood. Prioritize iron at every meal: fortified oats at breakfast, lentil soup at lunch, lean meat at dinner. Avoid black tea or coffee with meals — they block iron absorption by up to 60%.

exercise.science: Complete rest increases fatigue and stiffness over time. Short, low-intensity movement (10-20 min walks) boosts circulation and mitochondrial energy production without depleting reserves.
versions: When you're exhausted, even 10 minutes of slow walking beats staying in bed all day. Movement boosts circulation, gets oxygen to your cells, and releases endorphins — the natural energy boost you need without depleting your already-low reserves.

You don't need the gym today — you need movement medicine. A 15-minute gentle walk, some light stretching, or a slow yoga flow is enough to improve circulation and reduce the heavy-limb feeling of period fatigue.

Honor your exhaustion — but don't be fully sedentary. A gentle 10-minute walk or 5 minutes of deep breathing exercises increases oxygen delivery to muscles and helps shake the fog. That's enough for today.

selfcare.science: Sleep quality drops pre-period due to low progesterone and pain disruption. Maintaining an 8-hour sleep window supports iron-synthesis pathways and reduces inflammatory markers.
versions: Your body repairs and rebuilds during sleep — especially important when you're losing iron. Aim for 8+ hours tonight. Warm chamomile tea before bed promotes sleep by increasing glycine, a relaxing amino acid. Avoid screens an hour before.

Prioritize sleep above everything else today. Iron replenishment, immune function, and mood regulation all happen during rest. A warm bath before bed lowers core body temperature (triggering sleep signals) and soothes cramps simultaneously.

Rest is not laziness today — it's medicine. Your body is doing significant work. Sleep 8-9 hours if you can, and consider a 20-minute afternoon nap. Even short rest periods reduce inflammatory cortisol that worsens fatigue and cramps.

food.science: Low estrogen in menstrual phase reduces serotonin synthesis. Tryptophan (serotonin precursor) + complex carbs increase brain serotonin. Dark chocolate boosts endorphins and contains magnesium that supports mood regulation.
versions: Low mood during your period is serotonin-related — estrogen drops pull serotonin down with it. Boost it naturally: eat tryptophan-rich foods (turkey, eggs, pumpkin seeds) with complex carbs (oats, brown rice) — carbs help tryptophan cross the blood-brain barrier.

Your brain chemistry shifts during your period — serotonin dips with estrogen. Counter it with tryptophan foods: eggs, seeds, nuts, salmon. Pair with complex carbs to shuttle tryptophan into your brain. A small square of dark chocolate also triggers endorphin release.

The sadness you feel is biochemical, not a character flaw. Estrogen fuels serotonin — and right now it's at its lowest. Foods high in tryptophan (the raw material for serotonin): eggs, turkey, tofu, pumpkin seeds. Eat them with oats or sweet potato for maximum effect.

exercise.science: Even 20 minutes of aerobic exercise increases BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor) and serotonin. Studies show exercise is as effective as antidepressants for mild-moderate depression (Harvard Medical School).
versions: Movement is a natural antidepressant — clinically proven. Even a 20-minute walk outside releases endorphins and serotonin. Natural light exposure also regulates your circadian rhythm, which directly impacts mood. Gentle and outdoors is the goal today.

When you're feeling low, exercise feels like the last thing you want — but it's the most powerful tool you have. 20 minutes of gentle movement (walk, swim, yoga) releases as much serotonin as a mild antidepressant. Start with just 5 minutes.

Your brain needs movement to make serotonin. A short walk, some slow dancing in your room, or a gentle yoga flow — any of these trigger neurochemical changes that lift mood within 20-30 minutes. Nature walks amplify the effect through light and open space.

selfcare.science: Social connection releases oxytocin, which counteracts cortisol and low serotonin. Journaling and emotional expression reduce rumination, a key driver of sadness (Pennebaker, expressive writing research).
versions: Don't isolate when you're feeling low — connection is medicine. A 10-minute call with someone you care about releases oxytocin, which actively counteracts the sadness. Even texting counts. You don't have to explain why — just connect.

When your mood is low, protect yourself from negative spirals. Light a candle, play music you love, and write 3 things that are okay right now — not 'good', just okay. Expressive writing reduces rumination and is clinically shown to lift mood within days.

Treat yourself with the same gentleness you'd give a friend who's struggling. Wrap up in something warm, nourish yourself with food you love, and give yourself full permission to feel low without judgment. This phase passes within days.

food.science: Prostaglandins cause water retention and gut slow-down during menstruation. High sodium increases osmotic water pull into tissues. Potassium counteracts sodium and promotes fluid excretion (Hello Clue, evidence review).
versions: Period bloating is driven by water retention — your body is holding fluid in response to hormonal shifts. Cut sodium (skip processed foods, soy sauce, chips) and add potassium-rich foods: banana, avocado, sweet potato. Potassium flushes excess sodium and reduces puffiness.

Bloating now is mostly water retention, not fat. What helps: drink more water (counterintuitively, dehydration makes you retain more), eat potassium-rich foods (bananas, avocado), and cut salt. Avoid gas-producing foods: beans, broccoli, carbonated drinks.

Your gut slows down during your period due to prostaglandins — leading to gas and bloating. Avoid cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, cauliflower), carbonated drinks, and high-salt foods. Instead: cucumber, fennel tea, bananas, and plenty of water help move things along.

exercise.science: Gentle aerobic movement promotes lymphatic drainage and reduces fluid accumulation. 30 minutes of walking shown to improve PMS symptoms including bloating (Medical News Today, evidence review).
versions: Movement is one of the best bloat busters. Even a 20-30 minute walk promotes lymphatic drainage — your body's system for moving excess fluid out of tissues. Skip the couch and take a slow stroll.

Bloating responds well to gentle cardio. Walking, light cycling, or swimming gets your lymphatic system moving, which reduces the fluid retention causing the bloat. Even 20 minutes makes a visible difference.

The worst thing for bloating is staying still. A slow 30-minute walk increases circulation, stimulates digestion, and helps your lymphatic system clear excess fluid. Bonus: the endorphins improve your mood too.

selfcare.science: Abdominal self-massage in clockwise direction (following colon direction) reduces gas and stimulates peristalsis. Peppermint and fennel teas relax intestinal muscles and reduce gas (evidence-based herbal review).
versions: Try abdominal massage: lie on your back and use your fingertips to make gentle clockwise circles on your belly (following the direction of your colon). 5 minutes of this stimulates digestion and relieves gas buildup. Follow with fennel tea.

Peppermint and fennel tea are evidence-backed for bloating — they relax intestinal smooth muscle and reduce trapped gas. Sip slowly after meals. Avoid lying down immediately after eating — give yourself 20 minutes upright to let digestion start.

Wear comfortable clothing today — constriction makes bloating worse. Try a warm compress on your abdomen, drink 8+ glasses of water, and sip ginger tea with meals. Ginger speeds up gastric emptying, directly reducing that full, heavy feeling.

food.science: Heavy flow increases iron loss risk. Vitamin K supports clotting (leafy greens). Hydration replaces fluid volume lost. Omega-3s reduce inflammation without thinning blood excessively.
versions: With heavier flow, iron replacement is the priority. Dark leafy greens (spinach, kale) give you both iron and vitamin K which supports healthy clotting. Stay hydrated — you're losing fluid volume too. Add a vitamin C source at every meal to maximize iron uptake.

Your body is working hard right now. Keep iron levels up with meat, legumes, or tofu, and support clotting with leafy greens. Drink water consistently — even mild dehydration thickens blood and worsens cramping.

Heavy days mean higher iron needs. Build your meals around iron: red meat or legumes as the base, leafy greens as a side, and something citrusy with every meal. Avoid black tea, coffee, and calcium-rich foods within an hour of iron-rich meals — they compete for absorption.

exercise.science: Moderate movement on heavy days is safe and beneficial. Avoid inversion poses (legs above head) during heavy flow. Low-impact cardio maintains circulation without increasing flow significantly.
versions: You can still move on heavy days — but keep it low-impact. Walking, gentle cycling, or restorative yoga are ideal. Skip inversions (headstands, legs-up-the-wall) during peak flow days as they can temporarily increase bleeding.

Light movement is safe and helpful even on heavy days. A 20-minute flat walk or gentle stretching keeps circulation going and reduces cramping. Listen to your body — if something feels like too much, it is.

Heavy flow doesn't mean full stop. Gentle swimming, slow yoga, or walking are all fine — and actually reduce prostaglandin sensitivity. Avoid anything that increases intra-abdominal pressure (heavy lifting, intense core work).

selfcare.science: Changing period products regularly (every 4-6 hours for tampons/cups) prevents bacterial growth. Iron-rich diet + rest is more effective than supplements alone for heavy bleeders.
versions: On heavy days, comfort and practicality come first. Make sure you have reliable period protection, comfortable clothing, and easy access to warm food. A heating pad on standby for cramp spikes can prevent you from needing to take pain medication.

Give yourself grace on heavy days. Plan easier meals (batch-cooked iron-rich soup is perfect), reduce your schedule if possible, and keep warm. The more stress you're under, the more cortisol you produce — which worsens cramps.

Heavy flow days are a signal to slow down. Prioritize iron-rich food at each meal, stay hydrated, and use heat therapy proactively — before cramps peak, not after. A proactive approach reduces the need for pain medication.
